source.content:
Ben Carter: compiling Week 3 retargeting learnings here + we need a call on next-week budget allocation by EOD so Tyler can update caps.

Ben Carter: goals reminder: optimize for *activation lift* (% hitting first successful request + 100k tokens within 7d) w/ guardrails (exclude active opps + enterprise domains).

Tyler Benson: dropping numbers (Jan 27–Feb 2, all campaigns w/ UTMs `utm_source=paid_social&utm_medium=retargeting&utm_campaign=lgx_w3_*`).

Tyler Benson: Spend: $8.4k total
- Segment A: site visitors (7d lookback) = $2.1k
- Segment B: signup-no-activation (signup in last 14d, no API key OR no first_request) = $5.0k
- Segment C: activated-but-no-return (first_request, no activity in 5d) = $1.3k

Tyler Benson: Platform mix: 78% LinkedIn, 22% X. (We capped Meta until we get cleaner exclusion sync.)

Tyler Benson: Performance (primary proxy = “activated within 7d” modeled off event join; *directional* until Mei confirms lag):
- Segment A (site visitors): CPC $2.90, CTR 0.62%, est. activation CPA ~$92
- Segment B (signup-no-activation): CPC $1.80, CTR 0.91%, est. activation CPA ~$38
- Segment C (activated-no-return): CPC $2.35, CTR 0.55%, est. re-activation CPA ~$74

Tyler Benson: Notes:
1) Segment B is doing the work. It’s also the cleanest funnel-wise (we have emails as a parallel touch though).
2) Segment A looks expensive + noisy (lots of doc traffic that never signs up).
3) Segment C has low volume, unclear incremental.

Tyler Benson: Creative split (top 2 in Segment B):
- “Ship your first request in minutes” (dev-first) = +22% CTR vs. baseline
- “Lower latency, predictable cost” (perf/econ) = better conversion rate post-click but lower CTR

Camila Reyes: thanks. are we confident segment B isn’t just capturing folks who would’ve activated anyway from email + console nudges?

Tyler Benson: not fully. we don’t have a true incrementality holdout yet per audience. but we *do* see higher activation rate for paid-clicked cohort vs. matched no-click in same segment (still selection bias).

Ben Carter: agree we need to treat it as “optimize to likely incremental” until we add proper holdouts. For next week decision: I’d still put dollars behind Segment B since it’s closest to our primary KPI and cheapest CPA.

Soojin Lee: messaging lens: Segment B should keep the “first request in minutes” angle + a *single* proof point. We keep getting feedback that “latency/cost/reliability” all in one headline reads like marketing soup.

Soojin Lee: can we standardize on one claim per ad?
- variant 1: “Fast time-to-first-token (streaming)”
- variant 2: “Lower unit cost via batching/caching”

Tessa Morgan: +1. creative fatigue starting on the dev-first tiles (freq is creeping up). we can ship 3 new variants by Wed if we keep layout constant.

Tessa Morgan: request: pick the primary proof point so design doesn’t try to cram 3 charts in 1 image :)

Ben Carter: propose proof point = “first request in <5 min w/ quickstart” + callout: “hosted API + dedicated/private when you scale” (small text). ok?

Soojin Lee: yes but careful on “<5 min” unless we’re confident it’s true for most. maybe “in minutes” (safer).

Jordan Blake: quick check: exclusion list is still synced weekly, right? sales had a couple “why am I seeing your ads” pings last week.

Tyler Benson: currently: exclusion sync every Mon 9am PT from HubSpot list (active opps + target enterprise domains). if we want more frequent, I need Isabelle to help automate.

Stephanie Nguyen: weekly is probably not enough if we’re scaling spend. can we at least do 2x/week for the next 2 weeks?

Ben Carter: +1. Tyler can you coordinate w/ Isabelle for Wed sync as well?

Tyler Benson: yep. I’ll ping Isabelle. (Also: we’re excluding known regulated domains (fintech/health) per policy note, but there’s still some personal emails at those companies we can’t reliably catch.)

Camila Reyes: budget: what’s the recommendation for next week? keep total flat or shift?

Ben Carter: recommendation:
- Keep total weekly spend flat-ish ($8–9k) but reallocate.
- 65% Segment B (signup-no-activation)
- 25% Segment A (site visitors) but tighten to product + pricing pages only (exclude docs/blog)
- 10% Segment C (activated-no-return) and treat as exploratory.

Tyler Benson: +1. Also I’d lower LinkedIn bid cap slightly for Segment A. CPM spiked in EMEA (likely too broad).

Tessa Morgan: if Segment A becomes “pricing + product only,” creative should match that intent (more performance/value, less hello-world).

Soojin Lee: agree. Segment A should be “production inference that doesn’t fall over” vibe. Segment B is “get to first token.”

Camila Reyes: ok with flat spend + reallocation. any concerns about cannibalizing lifecycle email?

Ben Carter: we’ll add a suppression: if user is in the email test cohort AND has clicked an email in last 48h, exclude from Segment B (where feasible). might reduce overlap.

Tyler Benson: feasible on X; on LinkedIn it’s trickier but we can approximate via audience refresh window + event-based exclusion.

Jordan Blake: and please keep the “active opp” suppression strict. we’d rather lose some marginal activations than irritate pipeline.

Ben Carter: acknowledged.

Tyler Benson: proposed next-week caps (starting Tue):
- Segment B: $750/day
- Segment A: $275/day
- Segment C: $125/day
(total ~$1.15k/day, ~8k/wk)

Camila Reyes: approved. do we need any new landing pages?

Ben Carter: no new LPs. keep sending to the quickstart + the “first request” snippet page. (Docs PR already updated links.)

Tessa Morgan: can we add a lightweight banner to quickstart: “Need an API key? Create in console” (so ad traffic doesn’t bounce)?

Ben Carter: that’s more product/docs. I’ll sync w/ Marissa/Nikhil; for now we’ll keep it in backlog.

Soojin Lee: action: I’ll tighten copy for Segment A/B split by tomorrow AM.

Tyler Benson: action: I’ll implement budget reallocation + tighten Segment A placements (exclude docs/blog) + set Wed exclusion sync w/ Isabelle.

Jordan Blake: action: I’ll send updated “active opp” export fields to make suppression more reliable (domain + known aliases).

Ben Carter: cool. I’ll update Week 3 readout doc with this decision + call out the measurement caveats (no incrementality holdout yet). :thumbsup:

Tyler Benson: also flag: attribution lag in EU region (events delayed ~3-6h). not blocking but FYI for daily reads.

Camila Reyes: great. let’s revisit mid-week if CPA swings >25% or if sales flags any targeting issues.

Ben Carter: done. (reactions: Tyler Benson added :white_check_mark: to “approved”, Soojin Lee added :thumbsup: to messaging split)

source.thread:
Ben Carter: We need a call on what we’re optimizing for in this sprint. Right now email v2 Variant B is showing +activation lift (first request + 100k tokens in 7d), but D7 active looks flat/slightly down depending on cut. How are we weighting activation vs early retention when signals conflict?

Tyler Benson: FWIW the dashboard is noisy bc the dedupe fix landed mid-week. If you look at cohorted D7 (signup date) and exclude the day of the event fix, it’s closer to flat than down.

Mei Lin: +1. There’s also a “weekend skew” effect. Some of the Variant B cohort signed up Thu/Fri; D7 lands on weekend where usage is historically lower. Need to normalize by day-of-week or at least compare same signup weekdays.

Camila Reyes: We should be explicit: primary objective is activation lift (time-to-value). But we can’t ship something that *clearly* harms retention. What threshold do we consider “harm” vs noise? (And who owns that decision?)

Ben Carter: Agree. The sprint brief says activation is primary KPI, retention is secondary/guardrail. But practically, we’re debating whether to pick the “activation winner” if D7 is down a bit.

Soojin Lee: Another angle: are we sure D7 is actually a product signal vs measurement artifact? If Variant B copy pushes people to run the hello-world snippet, they might “activate” quickly then pause. That’s not necessarily bad if they’re evaluating + will come back when they integrate.

Marissa Cole: That’s exactly what we see in console behavior too. Nudge gets them to create key + first request, but repeat usage depends on “next project step” (streaming, evals, usage dashboard). We might need a second retention-focused nudge rather than penalize activation improvements.

Paula Mendes: Please don’t pick a “winner” based on D7 this early if it’s not powered. D7 is slow + confounded by holdout size. Better guardrails: support tickets, unsub/complaints, and any severe drop in “returning within 48h” maybe.

Logan Wright: Data note: D7/D30 are outcome metrics; they’re lagging. If we want a retention guardrail that’s faster, we can use “second session within 24-72h” or “>=3 distinct days active in first 7d” as an intermediate. But define it now or we’ll keep re-litigating.

Ben Carter: Ok, so proposal:
1) Optimize for activation as primary.
2) Add retention guardrail as: no more than -X% in D7 active (cohorted) OR no more than -Y% in 72h return rate.
Question is X/Y.

Tyler Benson: If we set X too tight, we’ll never ship. With current volumes, a 1-2pp swing is within noise. I’d set guardrail as “no statistically significant negative effect” OR “>-5% relative drop” depending on base rate.

Mei Lin: Prefer “relative drop” with confidence interval. But we need a standard. Right now we’re eyeballing.

Camila Reyes: I want a simple decision rule for this sprint. Something like:
- If activation +3%+ relative and retention is within -2% relative (or not sig), ship.
- If activation + and retention materially down, iterate.
But we need to define “materially.”

Soojin Lee: Also, retention could be down because we’re activating the “wrong” users (low intent). Are we segmenting? Like: users who created key but never hit 1k tokens vs folks who hit 100k? The latter cohort retention is what matters.

Ben Carter: Yes. Segmenting by “activated depth” is in the readout. Variant B increases “first request” a lot, modestly increases “100k tokens”. Retention drop seems concentrated in “first request only” cohort.

Marissa Cole: That suggests our next step is to increase “depth of activation” rather than penalize the email. If they stop at first request, console checklist can push them to streaming + usage dashboard + rate limit guidance.

Jordan Blake: Jumping in w/ sales lens: if activation improves but retention dips slightly, not a huge concern as long as we don’t cause low-quality leads to flood SDR handoffs. Please keep the enterprise opp suppression in place for retargeting and don’t change handoff triggers without looping us.

Ben Carter: Totally. Handoff trigger remains “100k tokens + visited pricing” (or “requested dedicated” CTA). Not touching that in this sprint.

GrowthBot: Funnel alert: Activation funnel event volume increased +18% WoW in `us-east` after email v2 rollout. No anomalies detected in error rate. (dashboard: https://looker.redwoodinference.com/dashboards/activation_funnel)

Tyler Benson: Another confound: Variant B has higher click-through to docs, but those sessions might not show as “active usage” if they don’t make an API call. D7 active is calls-based. Maybe we need a “docs engaged” metric too.

Logan Wright: Caution: if we broaden “retention” to docs engagement it becomes easier to game. But as a diagnostic, sure. For decisioning, stick to product usage events.

Paula Mendes: +1. Also check whether we’re counting D7 active on *UTC day boundaries*; EU users get weirdly bucketed. There was a note last week about region-specific missing events.

Mei Lin: I can sanity check cohort bucketing (local vs UTC) + redo D7 using rolling 168h window since signup.

Ben Carter: That would help. The debate right now is: do we pick Variant B today or extend test. Deliverability is stable, complaints low.

Camila Reyes: Extend by a few days if it meaningfully reduces ambiguity. But don’t let perfect be the enemy of shipping. We need a decision by end of week for Week 4 readout.

Soojin Lee: If we extend, please don’t keep iterating copy mid-test. We already had “tiny edits” sneak in last time. :grimacing:

Tyler Benson: +1. Freeze the variants; only change ops things (throttle) if needed.

Ben Carter: OK: freeze variants, extend through next Tue to cover weekend effect. In parallel, define guardrail: I’m leaning “activation is primary; retention guardrail is no worse than -5% relative in 72h return rate OR -5% relative D7 active (cohorted), unless CI crosses 0 meaningfully.”

Logan Wright: Suggest slightly tighter on 72h return (it’s higher N, faster): -3% relative. D7 can be -5%.

Mei Lin: Works. I’ll compute baseline variance so we’re not picking numbers out of thin air.

Marissa Cole: Can we align that these guardrails apply to console nudges too? We saw CTR up but a tiny dip in “second request” for one nudge variant.

Ben Carter: Yes, same principle: activation steps completion is primary for nudge experiment, with 72h return as guardrail.

Jordan Blake: Please also add a “support load” guardrail. If we cause more “API key missing” or confusion, support gets hit and enterprise deals get spooked.

Paula Mendes: We already have support ticket volume + “didn’t receive email” complaints in the risk log. Can set threshold (e.g., +20% above baseline in related tickets) triggers rollback.

Camila Reyes: Good. Summary:
- Primary: activation lift.
- Guardrails: 72h return, D7 active (cohort), support load, unsub/complaints.
- Decision rule documented in Week 4 readout.
Ben, please write this into the readout template so we stop debating each thread. :thumbsup:

Ben Carter: Will do. I’ll post a snippet in the sprint channel and link it in PM-651.

Soojin Lee: And I’ll review language so we don’t accidentally message “growth at all costs.” We should frame as “faster time-to-value while preserving healthy early usage.”

Tyler Benson: I’ll update the Looker view with 72h return breakdown + weekday normalization.

Mei Lin: I’ll rerun D7 with rolling-window method and annotate the dedupe-fix boundary in the chart.

Ben Carter: Thanks all. Locking on this so we can actually ship.

source.content:
Ben Carter: Dropping the first pass retargeting creative drafts here — would love feedback today so we can get exports queued for launch.
Figma: https://figma.com/file/RWI-retargeting-kit/DES-327?node-id=214%3A88

Ben Carter: Context: these are for the 3 audiences Tyler scoped (site visitors, signup-no-activation, activated-no-return). We’ll mostly run LinkedIn + some Reddit. Landing pages are quickstart + “Create API key” console deep link.

Tessa Morgan: thx. Quick note: these are still pretty “broad enterprise” feeling. We followed the brief themes (latency/cost/reliability), but we can tighten.

Tyler Benson: +1 on the enterprise vibe. These look great but might be too “platform-y” for the folks we’re retargeting (devs who already hit docs once).

Soojin Lee: Agree. Messaging-wise, the “Enterprise-ready inference” headline is safe, but for retargeting I’d prefer something more developer-first and immediate.

Kenji Nakamura: Can you clarify what “developer-first” means for this set? Less brand/hero image, more product UI? More code?

Ben Carter: Yep. Request: can we get a smaller, dev-first variant set (like 4–6 total) we can test against the more general ones?
- Less “CIO” language
- More “make your first request in minutes”
- Bonus if we can hint at streaming/TTFT without sounding too benchmark-bro

Camila Reyes: +1. For retargeting, we’re not trying to win a positioning battle, we’re trying to reduce friction and get them to first token. The creative should feel like: “oh, I can just ship this.”

Jordan Blake: Also reminder on guardrails: please avoid anything that implies a “sales conversation” / “talk to an expert” in these ads. We’re retargeting self-serve cohorts and excluding active opps, but we don’t want to pull enterprise prospects into a weird motion.

Tessa Morgan: Got it. So a smaller “developer-first” set could be:
1) Code-first (snippet background)
2) Console-first (API key + quickstart UI)
3) “Hello world” / quickstart CTA
4) Performance proof but framed as “fast responses” not “#1 benchmark”

Tyler Benson: That direction sounds right.
Specifically: the current set has a lot of “Trusted / Secure / SLA” cues. Not wrong, but it’s not what gets a dev who bounced off the quickstart to come back.

Soojin Lee: Proposed headline ideas (approved territory):
- “Make your first LLM request in minutes.”
- “Low-latency streaming, simple API.”
- “Ship faster with predictable inference.”
Avoid: “enterprise-grade”, “talk to sales”, “platform transformation”, etc.

Kenji Nakamura: For the code-first option: we can do a simple “curl” + “stream: true” vibe. Not too long.
Something like:
`curl https://api.redwood...` 
`{"stream":true}`
And a subtle “Get an API key” CTA.

Ben Carter: Love that.
Also can we keep the CTA consistent across the dev-first set? Prefer “Get API key” or “Run quickstart” vs “Learn more”.

Tyler Benson: +1. “Learn more” underperforms for retargeting in my experience.

Tessa Morgan: One constraint: LinkedIn text limits are annoying, but we can make the CTA button “Sign up” while the creative says “Get API key”. That’s usually fine.

Camila Reyes: If we have to pick one primary CTA for the dev-first set, I’d choose “Run the quickstart” (and the landing page can immediately show API key + minimal snippet). Second option is “Get API key”.

Ben Carter: Ok. For audiences:
- signup-no-activation: “Get API key” / “Run quickstart”
- activated-no-return: something like “Turn on streaming” / “See usage + cost breakdown” (but keep it simple)

Soojin Lee: On “activated-no-return,” we should be careful not to introduce too many features. One crisp promise: “Faster + cheaper with batching/caching” might be too much. “Track usage + latency in console” is straightforward.

Tyler Benson: Another thought: can we include one dev-first variant that’s literally “Copy/paste → first token” (not those exact words) but the idea is minimal effort.

Kenji Nakamura: Yep. We can do:
Headline: “Copy, paste, ship.”
Sub: “Hosted LLM inference with streaming + simple SDKs.”
Visual: tiny code block + minimal UI pill.

Soojin Lee: “Copy, paste, ship.” is borderline but I’m okay with it if the subline grounds it. Maybe “Copy, paste, first token.” is too meme-y.

Ben Carter: Let’s do “Copy, paste, ship.” as one variant. Keep the rest more literal.

Tessa Morgan: Action plan:
- I’ll spin a “developer-first” page in the Figma file with 6 variants max.
- We’ll keep sizes: 1200x628 + 1080x1080 + 300x250 (so we’re covered).
- Kenji will do code/console comps.

Tyler Benson: Works. Can you label the frames with audience mapping + UTM landing page suggestion (quickstart vs console API key deep link) so I don’t mess it up in ads manager?

Tessa Morgan: Yep.

Ben Carter: Timing: if we can have the dev-first set by tomorrow noon PT, we can still hit the Week 3 launch window.

Kenji Nakamura: I can have first pass in ~2–3 hours. Will @ you in Figma for copy checks.

Soojin Lee: I’ll review copy async. Please keep “developer-first” = concrete (quickstart, API key, streaming) and aligned to our approved phrasing.

Jordan Blake: And again: no “Book a demo” anywhere in these. :pray:

Ben Carter: :thumbsup: thank you all. Tessa/Kenji — tag me when the new frames are ready and I’ll route for final approval + exports.

source.messages:
Tyler Benson: Need to lock UTM conventions + landing page mapping for the retargeting launch. Last chance before I bake into HubSpot + ad platform templates.

Ben Carter: yep let’s do it. biggest thing: keep campaigns readable in dashboard + avoid 17 variants. what’s your proposed schema?

Tyler Benson: Proposal (consistent across LinkedIn/Google/Reddit):
- `utm_source`: `linkedin` | `google` | `reddit`
- `utm_medium`: `paid_social` (LI/Reddit) | `paid_search` (Google)
- `utm_campaign`: `ri_retgt_q1_2026_activation` (one umbrella)
- `utm_content`: `{audience}-{creative}-{cta}` (no spaces, dashes ok)
- `utm_term`: only for Google (keyword)
Also add `utm_id` optional later; not required for v1.

Soojin Lee: +1 on readable. Can we standardize audience names so they match our internal segments? i.e., `signup_no_activation` vs `signup-no-activation` etc.

Tyler Benson: Yep. Canonical audience slugs:
1) `site_visitor` (docs + marketing site)
2) `signup_no_activation` (signed up, no first successful request)
3) `activated_no_return` (first success, no activity in 7d)
Creative slugs: `perf` (latency), `cost`, `reliability`, `devex`.
CTA slugs: `quickstart`, `console`, `pricing`.
Example: `utm_content=signup_no_activation-devex-quickstart`

Camila Reyes: Works. Keep `utm_campaign` stable so we can roll up spend/CPA. If we need sub-campaigns later we can do it with `utm_content`.

Jordan Blake: Quick check: landing pages must NOT send enterprise prospects to “Talk to sales” flow via retargeting. We said guardrail is activation-first + exclude active opps.

Tyler Benson: Yep landing page mapping below (activation-first). Also I’ll add exclusions via CRM list + domain suppression. Isabelle — can you confirm the exclusion list format is ready weekly?

Isabelle Laurent: Yes. Weekly export (active opps + “do not market” accounts) to the suppression list. I’ll send you the automation spec after this thread.

Ben Carter: landing pages: can we keep it to 2 pages max? attribution gets messy when we split too much.

Tyler Benson: Proposed mapping:
- Audience `site_visitor` -> `/docs/quickstart` (with “Hello world” snippet + sign-up CTA)
- Audience `signup_no_activation` -> `/console/api-keys` (deep link; if not logged in, redirects to login/signup then back)
- Audience `activated_no_return` -> `/console/usage` (show usage + “enable streaming” nudge + examples)
If we need a non-console option for `site_visitor` on mobile, fallback to `/docs/quickstart?ref=retgt`.

Tessa Morgan: Design note: if we’re sending to `/console/api-keys`, make sure the first screen has context for new users (not just empty state). Otherwise ads will look “broken”. We can add a small top banner behind a flag.

Ben Carter: +1. We have the nudge component already; can reuse with copy like “Create an API key to make your first request”.

Soojin Lee: Copy consistency: for `site_visitor` ads landing on docs quickstart, we should mirror phrasing: “First request in <5 minutes” + “predictable latency/cost”. Avoid “enterprise-grade” wording in these.

Tyler Benson: Great. Locking conventions:
- `utm_campaign=ri_retgt_q1_2026_activation`
- `utm_content={audience}-{creative}-{cta}` using the slugs above
- Landing pages per mapping above (console deep links for post-signup audiences)
I’ll update the retargeting sheet + HubSpot tracking + send final template links.

Camila Reyes: Approved. Tyler please post the final “UTM + LP mapping” in the launch brief thread too.

Tyler Benson: Will do. :thumbsup: (and I’ll add validation in the dashboard so any weird UTMs get flagged)

Ben Carter: :shipit:
